segments.title: Why the Gorgon class suited coastal defence
segments.summary: The Gorgon class combined shallow draught, armour, and heavy guns for a coastal-defence role rather than conventional fleet manoeuvre.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke presents Gorgon and Glatton as unusually capable coastal-defence ships that could protect a shoreline with naval gunfire. Their design traded speed for armour and firepower, because an enemy approaching a defended fjord or coast had to come within range of them. Mature triple-expansion machinery gave the class useful range and reliability without pretending to make it fast.

evidence.note: The episode explains the class's shallow draught, protection, armament, and speed-armour-firepower trade-off.

segments.title: Armament arranged for a fjord fight
segments.summary: Gorgon’s single 9.2-inch and six-inch mounts gave it useful arcs of fire in confined waters where manoeuvre was limited.
segments.body: The episode uses the class's gun layout to explain why a seemingly old-fashioned arrangement could make sense in Norwegian fjords. Six-inch guns could engage moving targets rapidly, while the 9.2-inch weapons deterred heavier opponents. Wing-mounted secondary guns were valuable here because a ship fighting between islands could face threats from several directions without room to turn freely.

evidence.note: The transcript details the machinery, armament arcs, and armour of the Norwegian coastal-defence design.

segments.title: From Norwegian order to British monitor
segments.summary: Originally intended for Norway as Nidaros and Bergen, the ships were completed for Britain with revised armament and extensive torpedo bulges.
segments.body: Britain purchased the unfinished ships after Norway's order was suspended, then adapted the design for Royal Navy ammunition and wartime service. The 1917 additions included a large anti-torpedo bulge covering much of the hull, while removing torpedo tubes and planned 3.9-inch guns. The changes cost some speed but improved stability and protection without deepening the draught substantially.

evidence.note: The episode covers the Norwegian names, British purchase, armament changes, and anti-torpedo bulges.

segments.title: Calibrating Gorgon’s guns on German batteries
segments.summary: After joining the Dover Patrol in 1918, Gorgon tested its long-range fire against German coastal batteries before the planned autumn offensive.
segments.body: Gorgon worked up rapidly because the Royal Navy expected a major 1918 coastal campaign rather than an imminent peace. In July it fired at a German battery at about 33,000 yards to calibrate its guns, drawing a response from a German 15-inch position. The episode links this seemingly improvised firing practice to the wider pressure to bring naval artillery into the coming offensive.

evidence.note: The transcript describes Gorgon's Dover Patrol work-up and July 1918 calibration fire against German batteries.

segments.title: Naval fire support on the Belgian coast
segments.summary: Gorgon’s September 1918 missions show the demanding coordination, observation, and anchoring problems behind naval support for a coastal offensive.
segments.body: The planned offensive called for close fire support against the Belgian coast, a role that anticipated later naval gunfire coordination. On 28 September, wind, tide, and a broken stern-anchor cable left Gorgon with only its aft turret bearing on the target. Without aircraft to spot the fall of shot, the ship limited its fire to reduce the risk to civilians while German aircraft and coastal batteries contested the operation.

evidence.note: The episode explains the planned coastal offensive and Gorgon's constrained fire-support mission off Belgium.

segments.title: Reconnaissance under German coastal-battery fire
segments.summary: A close reconnaissance mission confirmed that German forces still held the Belgian coast strongly, even as Gorgon briefly exceeded its trial speed under fire.
segments.body: On 14 October, Vice Admiral Keyes took Gorgon and a destroyer close enough to test whether the German coastal position had weakened. Tirpitz and riverside batteries quickly showed that it had not, forcing the ships to turn away at full speed. Near misses produced splinter damage, but the episode uses the action to show why naval commanders valued a well-protected platform for dangerous coastal observation.

evidence.note: The transcript recounts Gorgon's bombardment and reconnaissance mission under German coastal-battery fire.

segments.title: How Glatton’s magazine fire exposed construction failures
segments.summary: Gl​atton’s brief service ended in a magazine explosion, prompting an investigation into insulation, missing cork, newspaper packing, and faulty riveting.
segments.body: Gl​atton was lost only weeks after commissioning when fire reached a six-inch magazine positioned near the boiler and engine rooms. The intended layers of cork, wood, and cooling arrangements were meant to isolate heat, but later examination found missing cork replaced with folded newspaper and gaps where rivets were absent. Dr. Clarke treats the disaster as a systems failure in which insulation defects, airflow, embers, and cordite danger could reinforce one another.

evidence.note: The episode discusses Glatton's explosion and the later findings about insulation, packing, and riveting.

segments.title: Could a retained Gorgon have strengthened Narvik?
segments.summary: The episode weighs treaty limits, dry-dock constraints, and a hypothetical 1940 Norwegian Gorgon against the German attack on Narvik.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke first explains why Britain and Norway had practical reasons not to retain the ship after the First World War, including treaty limits and Norway's dry-dock capacity. He then tests a conditional Narvik scenario in which a 9.2-inch, six-inch-gunned, heavily bulged coastal-defence ship could resist longer, signal earlier, and complicate German destroyer operations. The argument is deliberately cautious: it might not stop the assault, but greater resistance could alter warning, losses, and the options available to later British forces.

evidence.note: The transcript considers retention, a Far East role, and the possible effect of a Gorgon-class ship at Narvik in 1940.
